title: Polymer-in-ionic-liquid electrolytes
abstract:
Polymer-in-Ionic-Liquid Electrolytes
Churat Tiyapiboonchaiya,1 Douglas Robert MacFarlane,*1 Jiazeng Sun,2 Maria Forsyth2
1School of Chemistry, Monash University, Wellington Road, Clayton, Victoria 3800, Australia
Fax: (þ61) 399054597; E-mail: d.macfarlane@sci.monash.edu.au
2School of Materials Engineering, Monash University, Wellington Road, Clayton, Victoria 3800, Australia
Keywords: gels; ionic liquid; 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(triﬂuoromethyl sulfonyl)amide; trimethyl butyl ammonium
bis(triﬂuoromethane sulfonyl)amide; polyelectrolytes
Introduction
Traditional polymer electrolytes are solutions of electrolyte
salts in polymers.[1] Presently, the highest conductivities
observed at room temperature lie in the region of 104–
105 S  cm1.[2–5] The limitation in the conductivity arises
from the following features of these systems.[1] Firstly,
ionic motion in polymer electrolytes, compared to conven-
tional liquid electrolytes (s > 103 S  cm1), is coupled to
the local segmental motions of the chain. Secondly, in-
creases in carrier-ion density and mobility are difﬁcult to
achieve because the carrier ion generation occurs via the
interaction of polymer segments with the ions. However,
this results in an increase in glass-transition temperature
(Tg) with increasing ionic concentration; that is, the increas-
ed salt concentration causes a decreased mobility.
Recently, new polymer electrolyte systems have been
investigated where the salt is the major component and
the polymer has Tg typically around 100 8C, for example
poly(acrylonitrile),[5] or relatively rigid polymer. These
are more appropriately termed polymer-in-salt electro-
lytes.[4–10] The new electrolytes exhibit moderate con-
ductivity (s  106–103 S  cm1) and have suitable
material properties for use in a variety of electrochemical
applications. Angell et al.[6,7] discussed such a concept with
respect to materials described as ‘‘ionic rubbers’’, where the
polymer-in-salt mixture achieves good rubbery compliance
whilst still maintaining high conductivity. In these systems,
where the salt is the dominating component, it is suggested
that the conductivity is likely to be decoupled from the
polymer mobility. This coupling is conveniently quantiﬁed
in terms of the so-called decoupling index Rt. Rt is deﬁned
as the ratio between structural relaxation and the average
relaxation time for electrical stresses, and can be estimated
if the ionic conductivity of the material is known at the glass
transition temperature. In a polymer-in-salt electrolytes Rt
is thought to be quite large.[7] In contrast, salt-in-polymer
electrolytes (SPEs) are usually found to have decoupling
Full Paper: In order to achieve high conductivity in a
polymer
electrolyte,
polymer-in-ionic-liquid
electrolytes
have been explored. It is found in this study that poly-
[vinylpyrrolidone-co-(vinyl acetate)] (P(VP-c-VA)) in 1-
ethyl-3-methylimidazolium
bis(triﬂuoromethyl
sulfonyl)
amide (EtMeImþTf2N) and poly(N,N-dimethyl acrylamide)
(PDMAA) in trimethyl butyl ammonium bis(triﬂuoromethane
sulfonyl) amide (N1114
þ
Tf2N) produce ion-conducting liquids
and gels. The P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N mixture has a
conductivity around 103 S  cm1 at 228C, for copolymer
concentrationsupto30wt.-%.Thermalanalysisshowsthatthe
Tg of the P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N system is well de-
scribed by the Fox equation as a function of polymer content.
Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)/ EtMeImþTf2N gel
electrolytes were prepared by in-situ polymerisation of the
monomer in the ionic liquid. In the presence of 0.5–2.0 wt.-%
of a crosslinking agent, these PMMA-based electrolytes
displayed elastomeric properties and high conductivity (ca.
103 S  cm1) at room temperature.
Conductivity versus temperature in crosslinked PMMA/
EtMeImþTf2N gel electrolytes with different concentra-
tions of crosslinking agent.
1906
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2002, 203, 1906–1911
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2002, 203, No. 13
 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim, 2002
1022-1352/2002/1309–1906$17.50þ.50/0
 ratios less than one, indicating a strong dependence of ion
motion on the local polymer mobility.
Watanabe et al.[8] have reported polymer-in-ionic liquid
electrolytes that are made by dissolving compatible poly-
mers in ionic liquids such as 1-butylpyridinium halide with
aluminium chloride. The chloroaluminate ionic liquid is
hygroscopic and decomposes by hydrolysis, hence gel elec-
trolytes which contain other ionic liquids have been ex-
plored such as vinylidene ﬂuoride/hexaﬂuoropropylene
copolymer with 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium salts of the
triﬂate (CF3SO3
) and tetraﬂuoroborate (BF4
) ions.[9]
In recent publications from our laboratories and
others,[5,10] polymer-in-ionic liquid electrolytes have been
reported based on polymers such as poly(1-vinyl pyrroli-
done) (PVP), poly(–N-dimethyl acrylamide) (PDMAA, 1)
and poly(1-vinyl pyrrolidone-co-vinyl acetate) (P(VP-c-
VA), 2) and ionic liquids such as trimethyl butyl ammonium
bis(triﬂuoromethane sulfonyl) amide (N1114
þ
Tf2N, 4),[5] 1-
ethyl-3-methylimidazolium
tetraﬂuoroborate
(EtMeIm-
BF4) and 1-butylpyridinium tetra-ﬂuoroborate (BPBF4).[10]
N1114
þ
Tf2N is a hydrophobic liquid[11–13] which has low
melting point, high conductivity, and leads to good mecha-
nical properties when mixed with some polymers (the
N1114
þ
Tf2N abbreviation indicates the number of carbon
atoms in each of the four alkyl substituents on the ammo-
nium nitrogen. The addition of high-Tg polymers to the
ionic liquids typically results in a decreasing conductivity,
consistent with an increasing Tg.
Bonhote et al. and others[11–14] have synthesised and
investigated a range of hydrophobic ionic liquids with low
melting points (< 30 8C to ambient temperature) based
on various organic cations and the Tf2N anion. Of these
1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(triﬂuoromethyl sulfo-
nyl) amide (EtMeImþTf2N, 5) has the highest conductiv-
ity at 8  103 S  cm1 at room temperature.
In this paper, we continue our investigation of the
conductivity and thermal properties of electrolytes contain-
ing P(VP-c-VA), poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, 3)
or PDMAA in Tf2N-based ionic liquids, in particular
describing systems in which the polymer is polymerised in-
situ from a solution of the monomer in the ionic liquid. Such
electrolytes are of interest in a number of emerging elec-
trochemical device application including sensors, actuators
and, potentially in the presence of an additional lithium salt
component, lithium cells.
Experimental Part
Materials
P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N (mol. wt. 50 000, Aldrich) was
used as received. This is speciﬁed as a random copolymer of
composition 1.3 mol-% vinyl pyrrolidone and 1 mol-% vinyl
acetate. EtMeImþTf2N and N1114
þ
Tf2N were synthesised
by the methods of ref.[14] and ref.,[11] respectively. Methyl
methacrylate (MMA, BDH), tetraethylene glycol diacrylate
(TEGDA, Aldrich) and benzoyl peroxide (BPO, AJAX) were
used as the starting materials for polymerisation and cross-
linking of PMMAwhich swelled in EtMeImþTf2N. In the case
of PDMAA, N,N-dimethyl acrylamide (DMAA, Aldrich) and
BPO were used as the starting materials for polymerisation.
Sample Preparation
The P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N was mixed with EtMeImþ-
Tf2N at room temperature in different ratios. The PMMA/
EtMeImþTf2N gel electrolytes were made by mixing MMA
(10 wt.-% of the ionic liquid and monomer) with Et-
MeImþTf2N and polymerised via initiation with 0.5 wt.-%
of BPO in different ratios of crosslinking agent (TEGDA) over
a range of temperatures (63–77 8C) under nitrogen gas. The
PDMAA in N1114
þ
Tf2N electrolytes were prepared by mixing
DMAA with N1114
þ
Tf2N and then polymerising with BPO
(2 mol-% of the monomer) at 80 8C. Before measurement, the
samples were dried under vacuum (80 torr) at 65 8C for at least
3 d, after which they were handled in a nitrogen ﬁlled dry box.
EtMeImþTf2N was obtained as a clear hydrophobic
liquid.[14] The ionic liquid was characterised by electrospray
mass spectroscopy (MS), which conﬁrmed the presence of the
cation and anion in the positive and negative spectra
respectively and the absence of any contaminant ions at the
detection limit of the instrument (< 1 mol-%).
Characterisation
Conductivity measurement was carried out in a locally de-
signed multi-sample conductance cell consisting of a block of
aluminum into which stainless steel electrode compartments
were inserted. Cell constant of each compartment was deter-
mined by calibration before and after each sample measure-
ment with 0.01 M potassium chloride solution in water at 25 8C.
Conductivities were obtained by measurement of the complex
impedance of the cell between 20 Hz and 1 MHz using a
Hewlett Packard 4284A impedance meter. The conductance of
the samples was determined from the ﬁrst real axis intercept in
the impedance plane plot.[15] A Perkin-Elmer differential scan-
ning calorimeter model 7 was used to measure the thermal
characteristics of the samples at a scanning rate of 10.0 K/min.
Polymer-in-Ionic-Liquid Electrolytes

ning calorimeter model 7 was used to measure the thermal
characteristics of the samples at a scanning rate of 10.0 K/min.
Polymer-in-Ionic-Liquid Electrolytes
%Swelling ratios of PMMA/ EtMeImþTf2N gels were deter-
mined by immersing these gels (1 g) in toluene (50 ml) until the
equilibrium swelling was reached. The swelling ratio of the
crosslinked PMMA/ EtMeImþTf2N gel was calculated from
where W1 is the weight of the PMMA in the gel before swelling
and W2 is the weight of the gel after swelling to equilibrium in
excess solvent. We assume that equilibration in this context
includes partitioning of the ionic liquid between the swollen
gel and the toluene. Since the latter is in large excess the
residual ionic liquid content in the swollen gel will be negli-
gible. Samples were run in triplicate so that reproducibility of
conductivity, thermal properties and swelling ratio could be
P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N Electrolytes
The P(VP-c-VA) copolymer dissolved very readily in the
ionic liquid, EtMeImþTf2N at room temperature and the
mixture became a viscous liquid when the amount of co-
polymer was greater than or equal to 10% by weight. The
P(VP-c-VA) electrolytes were prepared at different ratios
of P(VP-c-VA) to EtMeImþTf2N (0, 5, 10, 15, 20 and
30 wt.-%) and conductivity measured over a range of tem-
peratures (22–70 8C). The conductivity of the samples is
shown in Figure 1. The temperature cycle was repeated
twice to conﬁrm the stability of the sample when reheat-
ed. EtMeImþTf2N has a conductivity of about 6 
103 S  cm1 at room temperature; it can be seen in Figure 1
that the conductivity of the P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N
samples steadily decreases from this value with increasing
copolymer content. The mobility of the ions in the mixtures
can be expected to decrease due to the increasing viscosity
and Tg of the mixtures as discussed further below. However,
the conductivities of the mixtures of P(VP-c-VA) in
EtMeImþTf2N remain quite high (  103 S  cm1) as
compared with traditional polymer electrolytes.[1,16–19]
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was used to de-
tect the Tg, crystallisation temperature (Tcryst) and melting
point (Tm) of the ionic liquid EtMeImþTf2N and the P(VP-
c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N electrolytes. Typical traces show-
ing the effect of polymer content are shown in Figure 2 and
the transition data are collected in Table 1. In Figure 2, the
Tg (onset) of the P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N electrolytes
increases slightly with increasing concentration of P(VP-c-
VA).This is in accord with expectation since the copolymer,
a relatively rigid polymer (high Tg), inhibits the movement
of the ionic liquid such that the mobility overall decreases.
The effect of the polymer on the ionic liquid can also be
dramatically seen in the disappearance of the crystallisation
and melting transition of EtMeImþTf2N from the thermo-
grams of the polymer-in-ionic liquids.
The relation between weight fraction of copolymer and
Tg of the P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N electrolytes can be
seen in Figure 3. This shows that the relationship between
Tg (onset) and weight fraction is close the behavior
predicted by the Fox equation:[20,21]
MeImþTf2N electrolytes as a function of polymer concentration.
DSC thermograms of 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 and 30% P(VP-c-
C. Tiyapiboonchaiya, D. R. MacFarlane, J. Sun, M. Forsyth
Here Tg is the glass transition temperature of the mixture,
Tg1 and Tg2 are the glass transition temperatures of the indi-
vidual components, and W1 and W2 are the weight fractions
Normally, this equation is used to predict the glass tran-
sition temperatures for random copolymers.[20] However, it
can be seen that the P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N electro-
lyte system is similar to a random copolymer system
because P(VP-c-VA) and EtMeImþTf2N are compatible
at all compositions, thus, a weighted average Tg is observed
as given by the Fox equation. The slight deviation at low
polymer content may suggest some polymer-salt interac-
tions which lead to a lower than predicted Tg or a lack of a
signiﬁcant effect of the polymer at low-weight fraction. The
P(VP-c-VA) based electrolytes discuss above are all essen-
tially viscous liquids which demonstrate the basic beha-
viour to be expected when a polymer is present as a minor
component in an ionic liquid, however, they are not directly
useful for electrochemical devices where electromeric
properties are preferred. Then the conductivity of electro-
lytebasedonPDMAAandPMMAcrosslinkedwithvarying
concentration of TEGDA were investigated as described
PMMA/ EtMeImþTf2N Electrolytes
The PMMA/ EtMeImþTf2N polymerised in-situ electro-
lytes were ﬂexible gels when the concentration of the
crosslinking agent was 0.5–1 wt.-% of the mixture. The
electrolyte containing no crosslinking agent was a viscous
liquid at room temperature. The 2 wt.-% of TEDGA in
PMMA/ EtMeImþTf2N electrolyte was a brittle gel. The
chemical nature of the crosslinking in these gels was
conﬁrmed via determination of the swelling ratio of the gels
in toluene (Table 2). It can be seen that the %swelling ratios
were constant for [TEGDA] ¼ 0.5–1 wt.-% and decreased
signiﬁcantly with 2 wt.-% of the crosslinking agent. The
degree of crosslinking depends on the inverse of the
Conductivity data for these electrolytes is presented in
Figure 4. Their conductivity values are similar to these
of the 10% P(VP-c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N electrolytes
(Figure 1). The conductivity of the gel electrolytes at
[TEGDA] ¼ 0.5 and 1 wt.-% are similar but lower than that
of the viscous polymer-in-ionic-liquid electrolyte (no
crosslinking, [TEGDA] ¼ 0 wt.-%). When [TEGDA] ¼ 2
wt.-%, the conductivity of the gel is slightly lower,
consistent with lower overall local mobility.
The Tg (onset) of the PMMA/ EMImþTf2N gel elec-
trolytes are nearly constant at about 92  2 8C as a
function of crosslinking agent concentration and similar to
the PMMA/ EMImþTf2N viscous liquid electrolyte (no
crosslinking). It may be that the degree of crosslinking of
the gel electrolytes is too low to inﬂuence Tg of the PMMA/
EMImþTf2N gel electrolytes. In addition, the Tgs of the
EtMeImþTf2N are the same, indicating that the effect on
Tg is not strongly inﬂuenced by the nature or size of the
polymer at this composition. However a signiﬁcant
b) Tcryst ¼ crystallisation temperature.
c) Tg ¼ glass-transition temperature.
Fox equation plot for P(VP-c-VA) in EtMeImþTf2N
Effect of crosslinking agent (TEGDA) on swelling ratio
of PMMA/ EtMeImþTf2N gel electrolytes.
a) Weight expressed as percent of total mass of mixture.
Polymer-in-Ionic-Liquid Electrolytes
interaction is indicated by the fact that the Tm and Tcryst of
EtMeImþTf2N disappear from the thermograms of the
PMMA electrolytes, except for the [TEGDA] ¼ 2% sample
(Tm and Tcryst ¼ 16 and 60 8C, respectively). It is
possible that the PMMA in this last electrolyte can not
completely absorb all of the ionic liquid because its degree
of crosslinking is higher than that of the sample with lower
TEGDA concentrations. This suggests that for practical
applications the nature of the crosslinked gel needs to be
optimised to achieve the desired properties.
Tf2N is a light yellowish liquid and has Tg
(onset) ¼ 81 8C, Tm ¼ 7 8C and viscosity ¼ 116 mPa  s
at 25 8C.[11] The conductivity of N1114
1.4  103 S  cm1 at 25 8C. This ionic liquid has higher
viscosity than EtMeImþTf2N (viscosity ¼ 34 mPa.s [14]).
EtMeImþTf2N has lower Tm, Tg and viscosity than
Tf2N because of the delocalisation of the charge
over the N–C–N moiety in the imidazolium ring, which
serves to decrease ion-ion interactions.[14] In addition, the
N1114 cation is rod-like in comparison to the almost planar
EtMeIm cation. Mixtures of PDMAA in N1114
homogeneous and transparent gels; the gelation appears to
be physical rather than chemical since they begin to ﬂow at
temperatures above room temperature.
Figure 5 presents the conductivity of the N1114
electrolytes. The differences in the ionic liquids also in-
ﬂuence the gel conductivities, the conductivity of the P(VP-
c-VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N electrolyte being higher than that
Tf2N gel electrolyte. Figure 5 also
shows a comparison of samples prepared by in-situ poly-
merisation of the monomer in the ionic liquid, with a sample
of the same nominal composition (20%) prepared by
swelling prepolymerised PDMMA in the ionic liquid. The
conductivity is a little higher in the in-situ polymerised
sample.[5] The difference could arise from differences in
polymer structure, including molecular weight and tacti-
city. Figure 6 shows the conductivity of the PDMAA/
Tf2N electrolytes as a function of polymer content.
The conductivity smoothly decreases with increasing
polymer content, showing the simple behaviour one might
expect when adding a rigid polymer to a ﬂuid electrolyte.
conductivities (about 6  103 and 1  103 S  cm1,

C. Tiyapiboonchaiya, D. R. MacFarlane, J. Sun, M. Forsyth
respectively at room temperature). The addition of high-Tg
polymers to these ionic liquids results in a decrease in
conductivity due to increasing viscosity and Tg. However,
the conductivity of these polymer-in-ionic-liquid electro-
lytes (  103 S  cm1 at 22 8C), remains higher than that of
other classical polymer electrolytes. The Tg of the P(VP-c-
VA)/ EtMeImþTf2N electrolytes follows the Fox equation
behaviour, except at the lower polymer contents. The
mixtures are very viscous liquids above room temperature
in all cases unless a crosslinking agent is added. The fact
that the ionic liquid supports rapid free-radical initiated
polymerisation allows monomer and crosslinking agent
to be polymerised in situ. The resultant crosslinked gel
electrolytes have good mechanical properties at the expense
of only a very slight decrease in conductivity. From a
practical electrochemical device perspective this holds
signiﬁcant promise for further development. It is also
interesting to contrast these molecules with traditional gel
electrolyte based on a high-ﬂuidity molecule solvent such
as dimethyl acetamide or ethylene carbonate/ propylene
carbonate mixtures. In many respects the physical and
chemical behaviours of these electrolytes are similar. The
key advantage of the polymer-in-ionic-liquid gel electro-
lyte is the lack of volatility of the ﬂuid component.
